steps.text: Determine whether the scope of experiments conducted at the facility were consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine whether the licensee’s procedures for the control and conduct of experiments are consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.

Determine whether the licensee reviewed and approved experiments performed at the facility, and any subsequent changes made to the experiments, in accordance with Technical Specification (TS) requirements and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Determine whether the licensee reviewed experiments for TS changes or compliance to 10 CFR 50.59.
Determine whether the licensee identified hazards associated with the experiments conducted since the last inspection and took appropriate remedial action.
Determine whether the licensee predicted the approximate reactivity effect on the reactor before conducting the experiment.
Determine whether the licensee confirmed the reactivity effect as required by the TS.

Determine whether the licensee accounted for irradiated items until disposing of them or until they decayed to an acceptable level, in accordance with regulatory requirements and applicable licensee procedures.
Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as required by the TS.
Determine whether researchers adhered to the licensees procedural controls when loading experiments into the test facilities and removing them from the test facilities.
Determine whether the experiments reduced the operator’s ability to control reactivity manipulations or the protective system’s ability to shut down the reactor if needed.
Determine whether engineered radiation safety controls were used during experiments and posting was accomplished as required.
Examine new experiments and changes to the licensee’s experiments and related programs since the last inspection.

preconditions.text: The facilities and materials being inspected must be licensed under 10 CFR Part 50 (not governed by other types of licenses).

constraints.text: Naturally occurring radioactive materials or those produced by an accelerator are not licensed by the NRC.
Reference to guidance is to aid the inspector in technical evaluation and is not to be used as requirements unless the licensee has committed in writing to use the specific guidance document.

steps.text: Review available documentation (Annual Report, experiment logs, safety review committee minutes, or experiment proposals) prior to the onsite inspection.
Review the licensee's procedures for the control and conduct of experiments prior to the onsite inspection.
Compare the list of experiments to respective license conditions, TS, Safety Analysis Report (SAR), Safety Evaluation Report (SER), and supporting documentation.
Review 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Directly observe 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Review records and conduct discussions with cognizant personnel regarding the new experiments or changes.

preconditions.text: Documentation is already available at the office.
It is practical to review the procedures prior to the onsite inspection.

constraints.text: Do not review experiments or changes to experiments that have been previously reviewed by NRC personnel.

steps.text: Review the information available to the operator that authorizes the conduct of a particular experiment to determine whether it has been reviewed and approved in accordance with licensee's TS and procedural requirements.
Give particular attention to experiments that contain special nuclear material (fueled experiments) or explosives.

constraints.text: Failure of experiments containing special nuclear material or explosives may result in a significant radioactive release and may be the maximum hypothetical accident at some reactors.

steps.text: Conduct experiments in accordance with the licensee’s administrative control procedures.
Monitor and understand the meaning of gauges that measure pressures, including vacuum conditions, where appropriate to the experimental setup.
Verify that items such as pressure gauges were listed in experiment proposals before the experiment was begun and remedial actions were planned in accordance with administrative control procedures.

preconditions.text: Before opening the experiment tube, ensure the primary capsule has not ruptured (which may be detected by observing a loss of vacuum).

constraints.text: Operators and experimenters must understand the hazards associated with an experiment and the methods to identify and respond to them.
If the capsule has ruptured, the opening of the experiment tube could be radiologically hazardous or cause a reactivity transient.

steps.text: Verify that the applicable TS limits for reactivity, such as shutdown margin, excess reactivity, and individual and total worth of experiments were not exceeded.
Stop the experiment.
Calculate the value to assure compliance with constraint limits and shutdown margin limits.
Verify the location and status of three past experiments performed under the license since the last inspection.

preconditions.text: If a regulating rod is changed by the established conservative cut-off amount

constraints.text: Control irradiated articles in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 until final disposal.
Make final disposal of irradiated articles in accordance with Subpart K of 10 CFR Part 20.

steps.text: Verify that the recipient is licensed to possess the type, form, and quantity of byproduct material to be transferred.
Transfer the byproduct material to the recipient.
Establish secure arrangements for the experiment to ensure that a single movement does not pulse a non-pulsing reactor, or exceed TS requirements or safety analysis assumptions.

constraints.text: The transfer of byproduct material must be done in accordance with 10 CFR 30.41.
Physical constraints must be based on experimental conditions, such as the reactivity worth of an experiment and the ability of the reactor to handle transients, as specified in the TS.
Inadequate physical constraints in a pool-type reactor may allow an experiment to escape and float to the surface, causing potential reactivity and exposure problems.

steps.text: Discuss the methods of handling experiments if removal of an experiment from the reactor cannot be directly observed.
Examine the process for consistency with the licensee’s administrative controls and procedures.

preconditions.text: Removal of an experiment from the reactor cannot be directly observed.

constraints.text: When an experiment is being removed from the reactor, it is assumed to be contaminated and highly radioactive until proven otherwise.
If the experiment has a reactivity worth of between 0.002 and around 0.006 delta K/K, it would normally be mildly constrained (e.g., resting on a support instead of dangling from a string or wire).
If the worth of the experiment is approaching beta (> 0.006 delta K/K), it should normally be fixed in place such that a simple movement cannot change its position.

Experiments with a worth of less than 0.002 delta K/K can be inserted or removed while the reactor is critical only if the expected radiation field for the personnel does not present an exposure problem.

steps.text: Determine how the licensee assures that experiments do not cause reactivity anomalies that cannot be controlled by the operator or control system, considering (1) the operator’s control over rabbit insertions and removals, (2) experiments that shadow the nuclear detectors, and (3) experiments that may interfere with the movement of a control rod or blade.
Verify whether the licensee has considered the use of engineered safety features such as remote handling devices, time, distance, and shielding in the planning and conduct of experiments.
Verify that access to areas where radiation is present is posted in accordance with 10 CFR Parts 20.1902 and 20.1903 and limited as required by the licensee's radiation protection program.

constraints.text: Apparatus and mechanisms other than controls, the operation of which may affect the reactivity or power level of a reactor, must be manipulated only with the knowledge and consent of a licensed operator or SRO (10 CFR 50.54 (j)).
Engineered safety controls for experiments must effectively limit occupational exposures to the levels required by 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee's administrative limits.
